figures.caption: Table 11. Using our checkpoints. Generalization results on two unseen tasks when merging ViT-B/32 models trained on six tasks.
Table 10. Comparison between merging methods between our checkpoints and KnOTS checkpoints.

Table 12. Using our checkpoints with LoRA (Rank-4). Per-task accuracy on eight image-classification benchmarks. We merge eight ViT-B/32 checkpoints, each finetuned with LoRA. The upper panel shows the per-task absolute accuracy of the finetuned baselines; the lower panel reports accuracy of merged models, normalized by their corresponding finetuned baseline (%).

Figure 10. Ablation study of the hyperparameter α. Normalized accuracy is reported for two model variants across α ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0}. Both Variant A and Variant B exhibit stable performance over a wide range of α values, indicating that the method is not sensitive to this hyperparameter. Unless otherwise stated, we use α = 1 for all main experiments.

Table 2. Per-task accuracy on six NLI benchmarks. We merge six LLaMA-3 8B checkpoints, each fine-tuned with LoRA. The upper panel shows the absolute per-task accuracy of the fine-tuned baselines. The lower panel shows the merged models’ accuracy, normalized by each task’s corresponding fine-tuned baseline (%).
Figure 11. Two-Task Trade-Offs on CLIP ViT-B/32. Pairs shown from top-left to bottom-right: Cars-MNIST, DTD-RESISC45, EuroSAT-SUN397, GTSRB-SVHN.
Figure 1. Effective rank across layers for an attention projection layer (e.g., query). The gap between ∆X stack and Rank-1 stack reflects merge-induced collapse.

Figure 8. Layer-wise Directional-Sensitivity Spectra (RAW vs. SVD). For each module and layer, we form the task-direction sensitivity matrix with entries Ji,k = ⟨∇fi(W ), Sk⟩ and plot the scree curve of its singular values as energy fractions σ2 k/ ∑ j σ 2 j versus component index k. “RAW” stacks rank-1 LoRA factors directly, while “SVD” uses a shared orthonormal basis. The two spectra are overlaid in each panel. A larger leading mass indicates stronger concentration of sensitivity into a few modes, whereas flatter spectra indicate a more distributed use of LoRA directions.
Figure 12. Preference Sensitivity under Random Global Preferences. Pairs shown from top-left to bottom-right: Cars-MNIST, DTDRESISC45, EuroSAT-SUN397, GTSRB-SVHN.
Table 4. Generalization results on two unseen tasks when merging ViT-B/32 models on six tasks.
Figure 2. Directional-sensitivity misalignment ξ(ρ1, ρ2). Larger ξ indicates stronger change in loss-sensitive directions when switching preferences.
Figure 9. Directional-sensitivity misalignment ξ(ρ1, ρ2) in the SVD basis.

Figure 6. Condition Number Anisotropy κ (RAW Basis). Layer-wise condition number κ(ρ) per module under the non-orthogonal LoRA basis (RAW). Larger κ indicates stronger within-preference directional concentration of loss sensitivity. (Here, ρ1 uniformly weights all tasks, whereas ρ2 assigns all weight to a single task (one-hot).)

Figure 7. Condition Number Anisotropy κ (SVD Basis). Layer-wise condition number κ(ρ) per module under the orthogonal LoRA basis (SVD). Larger κ indicates stronger within-preference directional concentration of loss sensitivity. (Here, ρ1 uniformly weights all tasks, whereas ρ2 assigns all weight to a single task (one-hot).)
Table 8. Eight CLIP/ViT-B-32 Models Joint-Task Results.

Table 9. Using our checkpoints. Per-task accuracy on eight image-classification benchmarks. We merge eight ViT-B/32 checkpoints, each finetuned with LoRA. The upper panel shows the per-task absolute accuracy of the finetuned baselines; the lower panel reports accuracy of merged models, normalized by their corresponding finetuned baseline (%).
Table 5. To further address concerns regarding latency coming from gradient-based optimization, we experimented our method with LLaVA [50, 51] on merging six VQA experts.

Table 1. Per-task accuracy on eight image-classification benchmarks. We merge eight ViT-B/32 checkpoints, each fine-tuned with LoRA. The upper panel shows the per-task absolute accuracy of the fine-tuned baselines. The lower panel shows the accuracy of the merged models, normalized by their corresponding fine-tuned baseline (%).
Figure 5. Effective Rank across layers for each module. Higher curves indicate broader subspace coverage. The gap between ∆W stack and Rank-1 stack reflects merge-induced collapse.
